document.title: ZIGN01D Hardware Suggestions
document.purpose: Structured hardware candidates for a proof-first, phone-oriented RISC-V ZIGN01D bench-phone path.
document.rule: Use certified modem/radio parts. ZIGN01D is the application processor OS. The modem handles cellular radio. ZIGN01D controls, logs, and debugs the modem through USB, UART, PCIe, or M.2.
document.north_star: RISC-V connects to external certified parts, proves interaction, and climbs toward a flashable phone-like device without fake phone claims.

--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.name: StarFive VisionFive 2
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.why_it_matters: Mature hobbyist/developer RISC-V SBC class with Linux history, USB, Ethernet, GPIO, HDMI, and serial debug access.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.zign01d_fit: Excellent first board for BOARD_JH7110_V0 because one SoC family can teach boot, UART, memory map, interrupt, and device-tree realities.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.risk: Not phone-shaped. It is a board first, phone dream later.

--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.name: PINE64 Star64
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.why_it_matters: PINE64 culture overlaps with open Linux hardware and open mobile experiments.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.zign01d_fit: Useful second board after VisionFive 2 if we want the same SoC family with a different board layout.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.risk: Board-specific boot and device quirks still need proof.

--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.name: Milk-V Mars
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.why_it_matters: Another board in the JH7110 ecosystem, useful for portability within one SoC family.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.zign01d_fit: Good later target once VisionFive 2 support is real.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.risk: Do not scatter to too many boards before one is sealed.

--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.name: PineTab-V
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.why_it_matters: Closer to the mobile form factor because it includes display, touch, and battery-style integration.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.zign01d_fit: Good emotional hardware target after JH7110 board support becomes real.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.risk: More integration complexity: display, touch, power, storage, boot chain, and peripherals.

--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.name: Banana Pi BPI-F3
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.why_it_matters: 8-core RV64 board family with more long-term performance potential than many early boards.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.zign01d_fit: Good second-generation performance target after the OS has board abstraction.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.risk: Not the simplest first bare-metal target.

--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.name: Lichee Pi 4A
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.why_it_matters: Known RISC-V Linux development board class with stronger compute ambitions.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.zign01d_fit: Useful later for performance and desktop-class RISC-V exploration.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.risk: Hardware/software support may be more complex than JH7110 for a first port.

--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.name: BeagleV-Ahead
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.why_it_matters: Developer-board culture and documentation discipline are attractive for a serious OS port.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.zign01d_fit: Worth watching as a documented TH1520 route.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.risk: Still not the first target before JH7110 is sealed.

--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.name: Milk-V Megrez
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.why_it_matters: Interesting performance direction for later RISC-V ecosystem work.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.zign01d_fit: Useful after the kernel has a real board layer and stable boot architecture.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.risk: Too complex for first proof staircase.

--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.name: Milk-V Titan
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.why_it_matters: Mini-ITX-style RISC-V board class with more PC-like expansion possibilities.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.zign01d_fit: Strong long-term workstation OS port target, less phone-like but useful for serious computing legitimacy.
--- compute_soc_and_board_candidates ---.risk: Not mobile-first and may require external GPU/display assumptions.

--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.name: Quectel EG25-G USB/M.2 module or dev board
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.why_it_matters: Known from Linux phone-style ecosystems and realistic for SIM, signal, SMS, and data experiments.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.zign01d_fit: Excellent for MODEM_USB_AT_V0, SIM_STATUS_V0, SIGNAL_V0, and SMS_LAB_V0.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.risk: Carrier band support, firmware behavior, antennas, power stability, and regulatory/certification details still matter.

--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.name: Quectel EC25 series dev kit
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.why_it_matters: Common LTE module family with many Linux examples and developer kits.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.zign01d_fit: Very strong first bench-phone modem if available in a clean USB dev kit.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.risk: Variant and regional bands must match intended SIM/carrier.

--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.name: Sixfab 4G/LTE modem kit using Quectel EC25/EG25-class modules
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.why_it_matters: Raspberry Pi-oriented kits can still be useful if they expose standard USB serial/modem interfaces.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.zign01d_fit: Good for early host-side Linux experiments and later ZIGN01D USB modem control.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.risk: HAT form factor may need adapters or USB mode rather than GPIO-first assumptions.

--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.name: Waveshare SIM7600G-H 4G HAT / USB modem board
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.why_it_matters: Useful for learning SIM status, signal query, SMS, GNSS variants, and modem command handling.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.zign01d_fit: Good MODEM_AT_V0 target if USB/UART use is straightforward.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.risk: Power draw, HAT wiring assumptions, and exact board revision details must be checked.

--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.name: SIMCom A7670 / A7600 dev boards
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.why_it_matters: Good for SIM, signal, SMS, and basic data-session learning.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.zign01d_fit: Useful backup path if Quectel kits are unavailable or expensive.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.risk: Band support, documentation quality, and carrier compatibility vary heavily by exact model.

--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.name: Sierra Wireless / Semtech EM7455 or EM7565 M.2 modem
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.why_it_matters: Common laptop-style modem path, useful once M.2 and USB-over-M.2 are part of the hardware plan.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.zign01d_fit: Good later for MODEM_M2_V0 and MBIM/QMI protocol work.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.risk: More firmware, carrier, and protocol complexity than simple AT-command USB dev boards.

--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.name: Fibocom L850-GL / FM350-class modules
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.why_it_matters: Represents the kind of laptop/phone-class modem complexity we may eventually face.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.zign01d_fit: Not first. Useful when the stack can already handle simpler modem control and logs.
--- certified_cellular_modem_candidates.risk: Firmware, vendor quirks, PCIe/USB mode, power, antennas, and carrier behavior make this hard.

--- best_first_pairings ---.pairing: StarFive VisionFive 2 + Quectel EG25-G/EC25 USB dev board
--- best_first_pairings ---.goal: cleanest RISC-V talks to certified modem proof
--- best_first_pairings ---.why: JH7110 board support plus USB modem AT commands gives the fastest honest bench-phone path.

--- best_first_pairings ---.pairing: PINE64 Star64 + Quectel EG25-G/EC25 USB modem
--- best_first_pairings ---.goal: alternate JH7110 board with open-hardware culture
--- best_first_pairings ---.why: Same SoC family keeps the OS port focused while adding board portability.

--- best_first_pairings ---.pairing: Milk-V Mars + Waveshare SIM7600G-H USB/UART board
--- best_first_pairings ---.why: Good backup route if Quectel hardware is not available.
Closer to the phone dream, but only after board-level truth is earned.

minimum_recommended.item: microSD card or eMMC module if supported

minimum_recommended.purpose: boot logs, panic rescue, serial debugging
avoid modem brownouts and USB power instability

minimum_recommended.item: Quectel EC25/EG25 USB dev board or Sixfab LTE kit
LTE antennas matched to module bands

minimum_recommended.purpose: later audio proof before I2S codec work

--- serious_future_carrier_board_directi.purpose: first modem control and data path

--- milestone_names ---.name: BOARD_JH7110_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Boot or board-identity proof for the first JH7110 physical target.

--- milestone_names ---.name: UART_DEBUG_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Reliable physical serial logs and panic rescue path.

--- milestone_names ---.name: BOOT_IMAGE_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Build a flashable image for one supported board.

--- milestone_names ---.name: FRAMEBUFFER_HW_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Physical display output proof on supported hardware.

--- milestone_names ---.name: INPUT_HW_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Physical keyboard or touch input proof.

--- milestone_names ---.name: MODEM_USB_DETECT_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Detect a USB modem and print vendor/product/device path.

--- milestone_names ---.name: MODEM_USB_AT_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Open modem control channel, send AT, receive OK, log timeout/error paths.

--- milestone_names ---.name: SIM_STATUS_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Run AT+CPIN?, prove SIM ready or explain SIM failure while masking sensitive identifiers.

--- milestone_names ---.name: SIGNAL_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Run AT+CSQ or equivalent signal query and report parsed signal state.

--- milestone_names ---.name: SMS_LAB_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Send a lab SMS through certified modem hardware with masked phone/SIM identifiers.

--- milestone_names ---.name: DATA_SESSION_LAB_V0
--- milestone_names ---.definition: Open a cellular data session through the modem in a lab setup.

--- non_claims ---: not a promise that every consumer phone can be flashed
not a claim that ZIGN01D implements cellular radio

--- governing_principle ---.short: Do not build illegal or uncertified radio hardware. Buy certified modem modules. Make ZIGN01D command them honestly.
--- governing_principle ---.architecture: RISC-V board or SOM -> USB/UART/PCIe/M.2 -> certified LTE/5G modem -> SIM/eSIM -> carrier network
--- governing_principle ---.first_real_win: A bench-phone: RISC-V board on desk, modem attached, SIM inserted, serial logs active, screen attached, ZIGN01D sends AT commands, reads SIM status, reads signal, and sends an SMS in the lab.
